After you they slandered me and seized my outstanding claims and sealed my memoranda. Why did you stand ready to obtain a tablet from the colony that is not your representative? The slave-girl, Hutruman your son, and the kuzita-textile and the house are held against the silver. Reckoned from the week of Innaya, son of Amuraya, they shall pay within 4 weeks. If they have not paid, I shall sell the slave-girls for a loss and if it is not sufficient, I shall take the rest from the house. Month

translation: My father gave it. Before Aššur and my gods I bless you. My son, just as you are painstaking for even every single shekel of your silver, take care to go for up to 1 talent of silver or more and seize the silver of the man. You hold my tablet stating that for your own authority you will seize my tablets. The man has treated my father and me with contempt. Be painstaking and do as much as you can to help me. He has caused my two servants to die and he will take away your silver. My message has gone to<gap> and you, send word to them there<gap>

translation: To Imdīlum and Uzūa, thus say Lamassatum and Narāmtum: The hardship in the City has become severe. What is this that you wrote? Why have they taken your father's house and your father's slave girl into ruin? To Uzūa: To whom do you keep writing? Your brothers are many, why do you keep writing that your brother holds your father's house and your father's slave girl for ruin? To Uzūa: To whom do you constantly write? Your brother is holding your father's house and we <gap> your brother will die in detention and we are dying of hunger in addition to our hardship. Come and save your father's house!

The investors are in debt in this matter of ours, and your father has laid claim. Urgent! Your father's house must not be lost. You have not arrived. Come and save it! No one is willing to guard your father's house. Come and set things in order! To whom can we look? We look to you.

Puzur-Aššur said: "Where are the 11 kutānum-textiles that I gave you?" Šu-Anum said: "I entrusted for 2 minas 17 1/2 shekels of silver for 3 weeks of what is available." Puzur-Aššur said: "The silver is not my silver." Šu-Anum said: "Of the silver that I entrusted which is available, I took 1 mina 10 shekels of silver. Take 2 minas of your silver."

translation: Speak to Mannum-balum-Aššur, Šu-Ištar, Zukuwa, Uṣur-ša-Aššur and Ikūppāša. Speak to Šu-Ištar: Your tablet concerning 16 minas 10 shekels of silver; your tablet concerning 12 talents of washed copper which you owe to me; your tablet concerning 12 minas of silver which the three of you owe to Puzur-Aššur. These three tablets of yours, the silver and its interest, have been left with you for the second time. translation: Aššur-nādā and Dān-Aššur seized us and they settled accounts before us. We settled their matters and they swore by the City that neither shall raise claims against the other, whether concerning a certified tablet, or other matters, or anything else. Concerning the settlement of their affairs about which they keep writing to you: do not settle any affair of theirs, and do not incur a debt of even 1 mina of silver on their behalf. Furthermore, if it is very important to you, let a report from you be left for me, and I will inform you from here.

translation: Tell Šu-Ištar, thus says Mannum-balum-Aššur, Šumma-libbi-Aššur, Bēlum-bāni, and Ilī-imittī; thus says Šumma-libbi-Aššur: Concerning the matter of the palace: after the report that I wrote to you, the man went out to the sikkātum and until this day we have not obtained a decision in the palace. The man has returned. Within five days we will obtain your decision in the palace and send you the final report. Do not worry at all about your copper in the palace; I will take care to release your copper from the palace.

Since the silver has gone for a single journey, he is satisfied with his silver and I shall be indebted to him with favors." Regarding the 10 minas of silver which the son of Manniya owes to Pilah-Ištar, the 10 minas of silver which <gap> owes to that same man, and the 1 mina of silver which his father owes you – let your instruction <gap> come to me and let me do as much as <gap> of your silver.

translation: To Aššur-mālik from Enna-Suen and Ennum-Aššur: The Prince is not here. The Chief of the Guard took 10 hand-textiles and 2 fine textiles, saying: 'My lord will come and he will give you a settlement.' And he has sealed the rest of the goods.

translation: To Zuška and Šāt-Aššur, Nāb-Suen says as follows: We are well. Do not worry about anything. we keep hearing good news. On the day we receive our affairs, he will send our well-being with the first available messenger. Do not worry.

translation: Aššur-idī says as follows: To Aššur-nādā: Make him pay 5 shekels of silver upon his arrival, my Asqud-dīya, and seal it together with the price of the donkey and send it to me with the first transport. One kutānu-textile is not recorded in my tablet; Aššur-taklāku is bringing it to you, it is my Asqud-dīya's.

translation: Ilaprat-bāni owes <gap> shekels of refined silver to Puzur-ilī. From the week of Šu-Kūbum and Kurub-Ištar he will add 1/4 shekel per month. Month Ša sarrātim, eponymy: hand of Iddin-abum. Before Aššur-šamšī, before Dadāya.

translation: To Pūšu-kēn and Aššur-nīšū; to Pūšu-kēn specifically, Ikūn-pīya says as follows: 6 donkeys and 15 talents of fine copper, half of it refined, half of it from Haburāta, and my servants were killed in the Titatum and they took away my copper. I have obtained neither a guide nor a donkey. Concerning my copper and the blood-money for my servants I am staying in Tuhpiya. I hear that I have been summoned to the City. Within one month a merchant will pay me 10 minas of copper; not even 1 shekel of silver fell to my hand, not even 10 minas of copper to my hand.

translation: 10 minas of copper fell to my hand and I cannot buy donkeys. You are my father, you are my lord! To whom else can I turn? I am poor. Sell either 4 or 5 donkeys for long terms just as you usually sell them, and send the donkeys to me so I can enter Burušhaddum and even one shekel of silver can come up for me. Look after Aššur's eye! And if you do not have any donkeys, take them with a brother. To Kulumāya: let your message cross over to me.

translation: Luapiš and Šupudahšu, his son, took the house in Garatum. Šumiawa, his son, divided the property in the house and he was made to leave his status as son. Since they separated him from the house, they are separated. If ever Šumiawa, his wife, his father-in-law, or his sons raise a claim against Luapiš, Šupudahšu his son, or his house, he will give 10 minas of silver and they will kill him. Atata, Inār and Humadašu are separated from Šumiawa. If ever Luapiš, Šupudahšu, Atata, Inār, or Humadašu or their wives raise a claim against Šumiawa, his wife, his sons, or his house,

Šumiawa and Šupudahšu are free of the Arhalum and Unušum dues. Atata, Inār and Humadašu shall not approach for Arhalum and Unušum dues. translation: From the 12 minas of silver under your seal that you sent us, we gave 7 1/2 minas 2 1/2 shekels of silver to the son of Ikuwa. 2 minas 6 3/4 shekels of silver are with Zuppa. Furthermore, Zuppa took 15 shekels of silver, saying: 'Ikūnum owes me.' 1 mina 1 shekel of silver is with Amurru-bāni. We gave 2 shekels of silver to Ememe. We gave 1/2 mina minus 1 shekel of silver on account of the slave girl. 1/2 mina 3 3/4 shekels of silver were lost either in travel provisions or in the tithes.

translation: To Šu-Ištar, Bēlum-bāni, Illil-bāni and Ilī-imittī say as follows: As for the 10 fine textiles of the votive offerings and the 2 Abarnian textiles that you gave to Aššur-ṭāb, he said: '10 fine kutānus were sold for gold. He gave me 1 Abarnian for 1/2 mina 3 shekels of silver; I sold one for 2/3 mina of silver.' Total: 1 mina 13 shekels of silver. He said: 'I owed 7 1/2 shekels of silver to him before Ah-šalim.' 7 1/2 shekels were deducted and 1 mina 5 1/2 shekels of silver and 10 gold beads.

translation: Šalim-ahum says as follows: To Pūšu-kēn: Concerning the tablet with my instructions that I am writing to Aššur-mālik and Hinnāya: if Aššur-mālik has already left for Kanesh, or if Hinnāya has not yet come to Kanesh, seize the representatives of Aššur-mālik and Hinnāya on the day you arrive, and let them listen to the tablet with my instructions with you. Then deposit my tin and textiles for terms with reliable merchants, who are as reliable as yourselves. Do not delay for one or two months. Your merchants must be reliable. 1 talent 5 minas

You are my brother; for the 1 mina 15 shekels of silver which I owe you, give it at the rate of 8 shekels each and satisfy yourself with your silver. Smuggle the tin! translation: To Puzur-Aššur, Šu-Ištar says as follows: Concerning my share of the colony's office, Itūr-ilī wrote to me for 4 minas of silver. My father, I have deposited 8 kutānus at 1/2 mina 4 shekels of silver each. In the price of my textiles from Šalim-ahum's transport in the colony's office, they indicated my name to Itūr-ilī. My father, let your report come to me as to how much reached the destination and I will send the silver. Concerning Zuppa's hand-textiles of which you wrote: 'They requested 8 textiles', I will send the price of the 8 kutānus with Adada. Either you yourself pay and get the colony's tablet,

translation: To Edinnāya, Aššur-ennam, Anu-bāni and Elāli, Ennam-Aššur says as follows: To Aššur-ennam specifically: We borrowed 1 talent of copper from the son of Asāya at interest and you were the guarantor. Later, you changed your mind and said: 'I will give you my sealed tablet for this copper and its interest.' Since the debt is our debt and not yours, you gave me your sealed tablet in the presence of 2 traders. Then you changed the contract and recorded on my tablet 1 talent of copper of which I took half the profit and stood for half the loss.

translation: To Šu-Bēlum and Kuzu say: Thus says Suli: 'My dear brothers, the very day you hear my letter, clear the slave-girl before you and sell her at any price, and take either wool, hides, or pirakannu-textiles from her price. Also, take care to collect the copper of the palace. To Kuzu: Do not come to me until you have sold the slave-girl!'

translation: I gave 40 minas of good copper and 2 shekels worth of small goods <gap> to Iddin-Adad at his disposal. 13 minas of good copper which I gave to Mazāya as capital, I also entrusted to Iddin-Adad. I gave 1 shekel of silver for small goods to An-Alī.

translation: Kukuwa, Peruwa, Huma, and Kubita owe 5 minas of refined silver to Imdīlum. Reckoned from the week of Aššur-šamšī and Amur-Šamaš, they shall pay within 22 weeks. The silver is tied to the head of the solvent and responsible one among them and their wives. If they have not paid the silver when their term is up, they shall pay 3 shekels per month per mina as interest. In the presence of Idī-Suen, Puzur-Aššur, and Laram-Su.

translation: To Uṣur-ši-Ištar say: Thus says Ikūn-pīya: 'You wrote to me: "Send me 10 minas of silver with Hunnia." Hunnia brought you 11 1/3 minas 3 1/2 shekels of silver. My father, my lord, on top of that silver you also give 10 minas of silver to go to the City with Hunnia, so that a transport may come up in your name. Also, concerning Suen-rē'ī, about whom you wrote to me: I have seized him once or twice, and he said: "I will send him one payment within 2 days with my servants." '
